skill.name: firebase-firestore
skill.description: Sets up, manages, and executes queries against Cloud Firestore database instances. Activates when listing or creating Firestore databases, configuring security rules, designing data models, writing client SDK queries, or checking indexes.
skill.summary: Guides setup, management, and querying of Cloud Firestore databases across Standard and Enterprise editions with multi-platform SDK support.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 737 potential issues with a computed risk score of 100/100, but all findings are false positives from documentation code examples. The 615 external_commands detections are markdown code fences (```bash) and inline backticks in reference docs showing Firebase CLI commands like 'npx firebase-tools'. The 9 network detections are legitimate Firebase documentation URLs and placeholder project endpoints. Weak crypto, system reconnaissance, and certificate/key file detections all appear in security rules teaching examples and setup instructions. One critical heuristic finding combining code execution + network + credential patterns is expected in Firebase documentation that teaches CLI usage, API endpoints, and service account setup. The skill's inherent behaviors (instructing users to run Firebase CLI commands, referencing Firebase URLs) are core to its purpose as a Firestore setup and management guide. No malicious intent, no prompt injection, no data exfiltration patterns found.

low_findings.title: Shell command execution instructions
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the AI to run shell commands via npx firebase-tools for database provisioning, listing, and creation. These commands (firestore:databases:list, firestore:databases:create, firestore:locations) are legitimate Firebase CLI operations inherent to the skill's purpose. No user input is interpolated into command arguments. Risk is low and expected for a Firebase management skill.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs in documentation
low_findings.description: Documentation contains hardcoded URLs to Firebase services (firebase.google.com, firebaseio.com) and placeholder project URLs. These are legitimate references for users to look up Firebase BoM versions, API documentation, and project endpoints. No data is sent to these URLs by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation code blocks misidentified as shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: 615 instances of markdown code fences (```bash, ```kotlin, ```swift, etc.) and inline backticks in reference markdown files were misidentified by the static scanner as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are standard markdown syntax for displaying code examples. All instances are benign documentation content showing Firebase CLI commands, SDK code snippets, and configuration examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Security documentation misidentified as weak cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple instances of hash algorithm references (SHA1, SHA256) and cryptographic terms in security rules documentation were flagged as weak cryptographic algorithms. These appear in Firestore security rules teaching examples and documentation about hashing patterns, not in actual cryptographic implementations.

content.user_title: Set Up and Query Cloud Firestore Databases
content.value_statement: Configuring Firestore databases, security rules, and queries across multiple platforms is complex and error-prone. This skill guides you through database provisioning, edition selection, security rule generation, data modeling, and SDK usage for Standard and Enterprise editions on Android, iOS, Flutter, Web, and Python.
actual_capabilities: Detect and select Firestore database editions (Standard or Enterprise) based on project configuration
Provision new Firestore database instances using Firebase CLI commands with location and edition options
Generate Firestore security rules following least-privilege and default-deny principles
Provide platform-specific SDK setup and query guidance for Android, iOS, Flutter, Web, and Python
Design Firestore data models with collections, documents, subcollections, and field type validation
Configure composite indexes and index exemptions for complex query patterns
limitations: Requires Firebase CLI access (via npx) for database provisioning and listing operations
Generated security rules are prototypes that need human review before production deployment
Does not execute queries against live databases - only provides SDK code examples and patterns
Cannot authenticate to Firebase projects without user-provided credentials or existing CLI login

use_cases.title: Provision a new Firestore database for a project
use_cases.description: Create a new Cloud Firestore database with the correct edition and location. The skill detects existing instances, recommends editions, and guides database creation with Firebase CLI commands.
use_cases.target_user: Backend and mobile developers starting a new Firebase project

use_cases.title: Write and review Firestore security rules
use_cases.description: Generate security rules for collections based on codebase analysis. The skill identifies query patterns, data types, and access requirements to produce rules that follow least-privilege principles.
use_cases.target_user: Developers who need to secure their Firestore database

use_cases.title: Set up Firestore SDK in a mobile or web app
use_cases.description: Follow platform-specific guides for integrating Firestore SDK into Android (Kotlin), iOS (Swift), Flutter, or Web (JavaScript) applications with proper initialization and dependency setup.
use_cases.target_user: Mobile and web developers integrating Firestore into client applications

prompt_templates.title: Create a new Firestore database
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a new Cloud Firestore database for my project. Help me choose the right edition and location.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new project that needs a Firestore database

prompt_templates.title: Generate security rules for collections
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my codebase and generate Firestore security rules for my collections. Make sure the rules follow least-privilege access patterns.
prompt_templates.scenario: Need to secure existing Firestore collections with proper rules

prompt_templates.title: Design a data model for an app
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Firestore data model for a task management app with users, projects, and tasks. Consider query patterns and indexing needs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning the data architecture for a new application feature

prompt_templates.title: Configure indexes for complex queries
prompt_templates.prompt: I have queries that filter on multiple fields with ordering. Help me set up the correct composite indexes and check for any required index exemptions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing Firestore query performance with proper indexes

output_examples.input: Create a new Firestore database for my project
output_examples.output: First, let me check for existing databases. I ran the list command and found no existing instances. I recommend an Enterprise edition database for native mode support. Let me check available locations and suggest one close to your other resources.

output_examples.input: Write security rules for a users collection
output_examples.output: I analyzed your codebase and found the users collection with fields: name, email, role, createdAt. Your app reads user profiles and allows users to update their own data. Here are prototype security rules that allow read access to authenticated users, write access only to the owning user, and validate email format and role values. Review these before deploying.

output_examples.input: How do I query Firestore from a Python backend?
output_examples.output: For Python, use the Firebase Admin SDK. First install with pip, then initialize with a service account. Here is a query example using async/await to filter documents by a field value and order the results. The enterprise edition reference guide has additional patterns for collection group queries and transactions.

best_practices: Always identify the database edition (Standard vs Enterprise) before following reference guides, as capabilities differ between editions
Review and harden generated security rules manually before deploying to production environments
Design your data model around query patterns first, then add indexes to support those specific queries
anti_patterns: Do not deploy generated security rules without human review - they are prototypes that may not cover all edge cases in your application
Do not skip edition detection when working with an existing database - Standard and Enterprise editions have different SDK APIs and features
Do not create composite indexes without first analyzing your actual query patterns - unnecessary indexes increase storage costs and write latency

faq.question: What Firestore editions does this skill support?
faq.answer: Both Standard and Enterprise editions are supported, with separate reference guides for each. The skill detects your edition automatically or helps you choose when creating a new database.

faq.question: Do I need the Firebase CLI installed locally?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses npx to run Firebase CLI commands without requiring pre-installation. Commands like database listing and creation run through npx firebase-tools.

faq.question: Can this skill execute queries against my live database?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides SDK code examples and query patterns for you to use in your own application code. It does not connect to or query live databases.

faq.question: Are the generated security rules production-ready?
faq.answer: No. Generated rules are prototypes based on codebase analysis. They must be reviewed, tested, and hardened for your specific access patterns before production deployment.

faq.question: Which programming languages and platforms are covered?
faq.answer: Android (Kotlin), iOS (Swift), Flutter (Dart), Web (JavaScript/TypeScript), and Python. Each platform has dedicated setup and query reference guides.

faq.question: How do I decide between Standard and Enterprise edition?
faq.answer: Enterprise edition supports native mode, collection group queries, and advanced indexing. Standard edition works for simpler use cases. The skill helps you evaluate your needs and provision the right edition.
